raw_response: {"rating": -4.2, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report/feature. It leans Left because the author’s own framing repeatedly attributes deaths directly to abortion bans, uses dismissive language toward exceptions and opponents, and excludes countervailing perspectives. The consistent directional framing and source selection support a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Headline and deck frame a concealed harm and retaliation narrative in the author’s voice: “The deaths from abortion bans you won’t hear about” and “Doctors may be even more hesitant… for fear of retaliation,” asserting both causation and suppression; (2) Early causal framing: the author writes that women “died from abortion bans after the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade,” explicitly assigning blame to the bans rather than presenting competing interpretations; (3) Emotive, sympathetic narration of a death — “walk out… safe and pregnancy-free. Instead, she died in bed, afraid and in pain” — heightens one-sided moral framing without balancing context; (4) Pejorative characterization of opponents: “anti-abortion activists quickly went into victim-blaming mode,” signaling the author’s evaluative stance rather than neutral description; (5) Dismissive language toward legal carve-outs: “so-called exceptions,” and a categorical claim that “the bans prohibit doctors from providing immediate care,” both minimizing or rejecting the stated intent of the laws; (6) Selective sourcing and omission: the piece platforms ProPublica, the New Yorker, ANSIRH, and Jessica Valenti while offering no perspectives from pro-life groups, state officials, or legal experts who support the bans, nor any data or cases suggesting different outcomes; (7) Lexical choice “pregnant people” aligns with left-of-center inclusive language norms; (8) Embedded related-link headline — “restrictive laws… taking us back to the Dark Ages” — is an editorial choice that reinforces a strongly negative, ideologically aligned framing of abortion restrictions without counterbalance."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report/feature. It leans Left because the author’s own framing repeatedly attributes deaths directly to abortion bans, uses dismissive language toward exceptions and opponents, and excludes countervailing perspectives. The consistent directional framing and source selection support a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Headline and deck frame a concealed harm and retaliation narrative in the author’s voice: “The deaths from abortion bans you won’t hear about” and “Doctors may be even more hesitant… for fear of retaliation,” asserting both causation and suppression; (2) Early causal framing: the author writes that women “died from abortion bans after the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade,” explicitly assigning blame to the bans rather than presenting competing interpretations; (3) Emotive, sympathetic narration of a death — “walk out… safe and pregnancy-free. Instead, she died in bed, afraid and in pain” — heightens one-sided moral framing without balancing context; (4) Pejorative characterization of opponents: “anti-abortion activists quickly went into victim-blaming mode,” signaling the author’s evaluative stance rather than neutral description; (5) Dismissive language toward legal carve-outs: “so-called exceptions,” and a categorical claim that “the bans prohibit doctors from providing immediate care,” both minimizing or rejecting the stated intent of the laws; (6) Selective sourcing and omission: the piece platforms ProPublica, the New Yorker, ANSIRH, and Jessica Valenti while offering no perspectives from pro-life groups, state officials, or legal experts who support the bans, nor any data or cases suggesting different outcomes; (7) Lexical choice “pregnant people” aligns with left-of-center inclusive language norms; (8) Embedded related-link headline — “restrictive laws… taking us back to the Dark Ages” — is an editorial choice that reinforces a strongly negative, ideologically aligned framing of abortion restrictions without counterbalance.
